inline_images.repository: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (via Wikimedia Commons)

inline_images.caption: Paul Laurence Dunbar, author of the 1903 survey of representative Black Americans, photographed near the end of his life.
inline_images.photographer: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division

related_chapters.subject: Lonnie C. King
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Evangelist Who Wrote on My Slate
related_chapters.why: Dunbar measures a representative figure by service passed to the next generation, and King traces his own organizing to his grandfather, a rural NAACP recruiter who shaped him early.

related_chapters.subject: Junius Williams
related_chapters.chapter_title: Pop Williams on the Front Porch
related_chapters.why: The essay holds up exemplary elders whose example, not instruction, shapes the young, and Williams describes his father as the role model behind his path.

related_chapters.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
related_chapters.chapter_title: Grandpa Ben Said We're As Good As Anybody
related_chapters.why: Dunbar locates the source of achievement in inherited self-respect, and the Ladner sisters recall a grandfather who taught them they were as good as anybody, a lesson that carried into SNCC.

related_chapters.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
related_chapters.chapter_title: Wilkins in Elegant Prose, Hooks in the Gospel Style
related_chapters.why: Dunbar's essay is a roll call of representative leaders handing the work forward, and Roxborough reflects on the succession of NAACP leadership from Roy Wilkins to Benjamin Hooks.

related_chapters.subject: Amos C. Brown
related_chapters.why: The essay celebrates mentors who lift up the young toward leadership, and Brown recounts being mentored as a teenager by Medgar Evers in the Mississippi NAACP youth work.

related_chapters.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
related_chapters.chapter_title: On Your Mark in Front of Jesse Owens's House
related_chapters.topic: Role Models and a Path to Ministry
related_chapters.why: Dunbar pairs religion and example across generations, and McKinney names the role models who set him toward the ministry, a Morehouse contemporary of King.

top_voice.subject: Lonnie C. King
top_voice.reason: Dunbar defines the representative man as one who achieves for the race and transmits the work onward, and King embodies that line of descent, carrying his NAACP-recruiting grandfather's example into leadership of the Atlanta Student Movement.

closing_image.alt: Graves Hall on the Morehouse College campus, Atlanta, Georgia
closing_image.caption: Graves Hall on the Morehouse College campus, Atlanta, Georgia. Lonnie C. King is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.
closing_image.license: Creative Commons CC0 1.0 Universal Public Domain Dedication

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Representative American Negroes raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, Amos C. Brown, and Mildred Bond Roxborough.
